And we would only let them buy the lowest markup gold. And people would realize, crew grants, all those are marked up. You can buy, if you buy what they call junk gold, it's highly marked up. We would only let them buy a little gold. And our concept was we don't want you to be invested until you're comfortable with the investment, until you see why people like gold, until it's in your hands. We did physical delivery, meaning we sent it to your house, until you understand the majesty, the history, the beauty, the reason besides just the economics. And when they bought some gold, we'd then let them buy some more. If they bought some more and they were comfortable, we'd let them buy silver. If they bought silver, we'd let them buy some more. Then we'd move them to very conservative rare coins. And then if they really wanted to, we'd move them to more exotic, you know, MS65. And I had, this is long, you'll remember this. I, we were the biggest user of MS, or what are they called? OS6, or whatever the big IBM automatic letter writers. And I had all these sequences. I spent all this time going through every scenario that a salesperson could encounter in every, every fork in the road. And we had books, it was manual, where when he put it down, talking to this gentleman, he said, well, I need more information, or I got to sell this. He'd look up the scenario. He'd see if we'd already sent a letter on that. He'd order it up. It was all manual card with a markup. We'd go there. We had all these, whatever they are, OS6s. We had auto pens that were signing things. But we turned it into a business that went from, the guy had $300,000 in gross commission in the year that I met him. And we did $25 or $30 million a commission 12 months later. And at one point we were doing $2.5 billion. Gold's not a real big markup, but it was a tonnage business. I did. Take a breath before your third one. I won't derail you. Yeah, okay, good. No, I'm just trying to think of things that are illustrative. I just want to make sure you don't miss it. First of all, it's a lot of things fast. And a lot of them are great examples of things we talked about today and yesterday. But the one we really didn't talk about that he just hit. Don't miss the counterintuitive idea. You talk about how difficult it is for the sales-minded person to put a delay factor in a sale. They restricted the amount that somebody could buy and told them, no, we won't let you spend that much money yet and buy that much. Well, I should tell you, our average aggregate value of a client was so off the wall. I mean, we had to wait. It was like delayed gratification, but the payoff over time was wonderful. And we had to trust. And we tried to operate with a more, again, it was a discretionary, not a primary investment. We tried to operate with a sense of a fiduciary responsibility. So we really tried to do it with integrity and intention. Probably. Now the third. Now the third. Well, I mean, there's so many things I've done. Probably a really interesting, I'll give you, because it has about seven variants in it. I did a lot of newsletter stuff. And I did a lot of newsletter stuff because a lot of these newsletter people were people who were, Amy had a job and they studied Austrian economics or they were investors and they started writing and then they started sending out lousy marketing pieces. And they were terrible. I'll tell you the four or five things I did that were quite interesting because there's about three separate lessons in it and it was pretty interesting. I just did 40K studies when I came, but I can't remember most of them now. I'm sorry. First of all, most newsletters would only mail if they made a profit. Don't ask why. They didn't understand Marshall Network. So I would educate them to that and we would expand dramatically our ability to mail. Number one. Number two, they didn't have good systems when they brought somebody in of redeploying the buyer ethically and so we created all kinds of other activities for them. Number three, once these newsletter publishers started making a lot of money, they became like drunken sailors and they spent it all. And it was in a cruel business that they were in a lot of trouble in the year they had to fulfill. So I made a fortune for them and for me by creating early renewal, reactivation programs and I'll tell you I got a couple of them because they were really cool. I get a lifetime, when the interest rates were 18%, we did the coolest lifetime membership in the world. Lifetime membership, but you could have your pro-rata money back anytime. You wanted because we were selling a subscription at a discount for 100 bucks. It was costing us six. We were getting, I think it was 1250 for a lifetime. We were earning 18% per year on it. So we did lifetime. Then I did five year. Then I did specialty clubs. Not like, you know, now it's very popular, but it wasn't 20 years ago.
